bordetella: This bacterial infection produces a distinctive prolonged coughing illness in infants that historically killed hundreds of thousands of children before mass immunization changed the picture.
choledocholithiasis: Sudden right-upper-quadrant pain, dark urine, and pale stools point to a blockage where a hollow stone-forming organ shares plumbing with a nearby digestive gland.
choroiditis: Inflammation of the vascular tissue behind the retina can produce floaters and blurred vision and is often driven by a systemic granulomatous or parasitic disease.
covid: A recent viral respiratory illness reshaped global public health, was famously first identified in late 2019, and became notorious for a loss of smell and taste in the early wave.
crigler: This inherited enzyme deficiency prevents the liver from conjugating a yellow pigment, producing severe jaundice from birth and sometimes requiring lifelong phototherapy or a transplant.
dacryoadenitis: Painful swelling of a paired secretory structure just above the outer eye can result from viral, bacterial, or autoimmune inflammation.
dupuytrens: A slow-progressing fibrous contracture of a palmar structure gradually curls the ring and little digits inward and disproportionately affects men of Northern European descent.
dysgeusia: Chemotherapy, zinc deficiency, and certain medications can distort or blunt one of the classical five senses in a way many patients describe as metallic.
dysmenorrhea: Cyclical crampy lower pelvic pain that peaks in the first days of a monthly physiologic event is often prostaglandin-driven and responds well to NSAIDs.
ecchymosis: A larger, flat, non-blanching skin discoloration from extravasated red cells appears blue-black at first and evolves through green and yellow as heme pigments break down.
ecthyma: A deeper, ulcerative form of a superficial bacterial skin infection produces punched-out crusted lesions, most often in children and immunocompromised adults.
epiglottitis: A sudden high fever, drooling, and a muffled voice in a child who prefers to sit forward is a true airway emergency once tied to a bacterium now curbed by routine immunization.
erythema: A general term for redness of the skin from dilated capillaries — the noun stem hides inside many named rashes tied to drugs, infections, and autoimmune disease.
fibrosis: The scarring endpoint of many chronic injuries — from the lungs of a coal miner to the liver of a heavy drinker — replaces working tissue with unyielding collagen.
kwashiorkor: A form of severe protein malnutrition classically presents in a weaned toddler with a swollen belly, reddish hair, and peeling skin, and is common in famine settings.
melanoma: A pigmented skin lesion that changes in size, color, or border can hide the deadliest of the common skin cancers and is strongly driven by ultraviolet exposure.
molluscum: A poxvirus produces clusters of small, dome-shaped, umbilicated bumps on the skin that spread readily among children and clear on their own over months to years.
mucocele: A soft, bluish, painless swelling on the inside of a lower lip after a minor bite is a common example of this benign collection of trapped secretions from a minor gland.
nosocomial: This adjective describes an infection acquired during care in a healthcare setting rather than in the community, and covers everything from resistant-organism pneumonia to catheter-associated bloodstream infection.
parkinsonism: A slowing of movement with a resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and a shuffling gait defines a syndrome named for a nineteenth-century London physician who described its shaking palsy.
periodontitis: Chronic inflammation of the tissues that anchor the teeth destroys supporting bone, and modern evidence links this common oral disease to cardiovascular risk.
plague: A rodent-associated bacterial infection with buboes, fever, and sometimes blackened extremities famously killed a third of medieval Europe and still occurs in rural pockets of the western United States.
polyneuritis: A symmetric burning-and-tingling sensory loss of the feet climbing up the legs suggests widespread inflammation of many peripheral nerves, often driven by diabetes, alcohol, or toxins.
pyoderma: A generic descriptor for a pus-producing skin infection also names a striking ulcerative autoimmune disorder that responds to steroids rather than antibiotics.
strangulated: This adjective, applied most often to a trapped bowel loop or an incarcerated tissue, indicates that the blood supply is cut off and warns of imminent tissue death without emergency intervention.
takayasu: A Japanese ophthalmologist gave his name to a large-vessel arteritis of young women that inflames and narrows the aorta and its main branches, sometimes producing absent pulses in the arms.
takotsubo: Sudden apex-ballooning of the left ventricle after an intense emotional shock mimics a heart attack in older women, and takes its name from a Japanese pot for catching octopus.
tracheomalacia: Abnormal softness of the cartilaginous rings supporting the main airway causes a barking cough and stridor that worsens with crying in infants and with exertion in adults.
ureteric: An adjective referring to the paired muscular tubes that carry urine from kidney to bladder, and often paired with words like colic, stone, or reflux.
whooping: A characteristic inspiratory sound following paroxysms of dry cough gives its evocative name to a childhood bacterial infection prevented by a triple childhood vaccine.
